upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a moist, nearly saturated profile with near-zero spread at 850mb (5.4°C/5.4°C) and 700mb (-2.9°C/-2.9°C), indicating a deep overcast with cloud bases near 1400–3000m MSL — consistent with the 100% cloud cover reported across all sites. The surface-to-850mb lapse rate is 7.12°C/km, below dry adiabatic (9.8), suppressing meaningful convective thermals. Winds veer from SSW at 16 kts at 850mb to W at 21 kts at 500mb, introducing moderate wind shear aloft; thermal ceiling is effectively capped by the saturated layer near 850mb (~1463m MSL), leaving very little usable thermal airspace.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for Bay Area. KSFO: recent light rain (RAE46), 9SM vis, BKN017–OVC036 ceiling, W at 9–13 kts. KHAF: recent -RA and BR, ceilings as low as SCT005/BKN013 around 2115Z, improving to OVC027 by 2155Z — marine layer active and recently penetrated by light rain. KAPC: recent light rain (RAE06B29E44), OVC022-026, SSW at 8 kts — inland overcast confirms widespread post-frontal cover. KRHV: BKN030, SSE at 9 kts shifting to SW at 13G17 kts — low broken ceiling over Ed Levin/Mission Peak area. KWVI: gusts to 21 kts, SCT014-BKN040, recent precip activity. Overall: low ceilings, recent precipitation, and marine-layer-saturated profiles across all stations.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin high launch needs a thermal or valley-breeze cycle to work; the ideal is a W to NW flow of 8–16 kts at launch with clearing skies to drive convection. Without thermals and with poor visibility or overcast, this ridge becomes a cold, wind-exposed non-starter.
sites.todaySetup: The decisive reason this site is NO FLY today is a persistent, 100% overcast deck sitting on the site all day — consistent with a saturated sounding, post-frontal cloud cover, and recent light precipitation across the South Bay. Without solar heating, there are no thermals, and the BL stays shallow (430–775m, only briefly spiking to 1275m around 2 PM under the cloud gap). Winds at 10m are running SSW at 10–14 kts all day, rotating slowly toward W-NW by late afternoon, which is directionally marginal for the launch but irrelevant without a sun angle. KRHV confirms BKN030 ceiling over the valley floor and recent SSW-SW flow with gusts to 17 kts. CAPE values are negligible (50–150 J/kg), and precip probability reaches 14% at 2 PM, suggesting residual instability. No viable flying window exists today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Solid overcast at 100%, winds SSW 10–13 kts at launch. No solar heating, shallow BL 430–565m. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Overcast persists at 100%, winds rotating SW to W at 11–14 kts, BL marginally deeper to 1275m at 2 PM. Precip risk 12–14%. Still not flyable — cloud base too low, thermals absent.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds veer toward WNW 10–11 kts, BL collapses back to 375–625m, cloud remains 98–100%. No improvement; conditions deteriorating.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast all day, no solar heating, shallow BL, residual precip risk up to 14%, low ceilings confirmed by KRHV BKN030

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, valley floor launch, SW-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft lower launch is a short thermal and valley-breeze site suited to learning days with gentle W to SW flow and light thermals. It needs clear skies and calm to moderate winds (6–12 kts) to produce safe, consistent air.
sites.todaySetup: Same fundamental problem as the high launch: 100% overcast all day with no solar input means zero thermal development, and the BL stays shallow at 560–945m until briefly deepening in early afternoon. Winds at this lower elevation are SSW at 8–12 kts, directionally acceptable but thermally useless without sun. KRHV BKN030 ceiling confirms the overcast is a real ceiling sitting just above valley height. Precip risk runs 3–12%, and with KWVI showing gusts to 21 kts earlier this morning, residual atmospheric instability is present. No training or recreational flying is viable today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast, SSW winds 8–12 kts, BL 560–675m. Completely unsuitable — no thermals, soggy air.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds shift SW to W at 10–11 kts, BL briefly reaches 1305m at 2 PM but under full cloud cover. Precip risk 6–12%. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds veer toward WNW-W at 8–10 kts, BL collapses to 410–715m, cloud remains 84–100%. No improvement.

verdict.hazards: Full overcast, no thermals, low ceiling per KRHV BKN030, residual precipitation risk

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site powered by the marine onshore flow. Ideal conditions are W to NW winds at 12–22 kts at ridge height, no marine layer fog obscuring the LZ, and a steady breeze without large gust differentials. The site does not require thermals but needs clean, organized flow over the bluff.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is NO FLY today due to a combination of wrong wind direction, low ceilings, and recent precipitation in the immediate coastal zone. Winds at 10m are running SSW at 9–12 kts — too southerly to generate usable ridge lift over a W-facing bluff, and in fact pointing air directly at the cliff face at an unfavorable angle. KHAF tells the full story: the Half Moon Bay station, just a few miles south, had 1SM visibility in light rain and fog as recently as 2115Z, with ceilings dropping to SCT005 (500 ft AGL), and the marine layer is still active with OVC027 at 2155Z. The NDBC 46012 buoy at Half Moon Bay shows SST of only 12.6°C — cold water supporting a persistent, deep marine layer. With BL heights at 475–720m and 100% cloud cover all day, there is no usable airspace above the ridge. Wind direction must clock to W-NW before this site becomes viable, and today that transition doesn't materialize.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: SSW at 6–11 kts, 100% overcast, KHAF recently reporting fog and light rain just to the south. Marine layer active. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds remain SSW to SW at 9–11 kts, cloud cover 100%, BL 475–720m. Wrong direction for ridge lift, marginal ceiling. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds stay SW at 9–12 kts, no directional improvement, cloud persists. Coastal marine layer is entrenched. Not flyable.

verdict.hazards: Wrong wind direction (SSW vs ideal W-NW), marine layer with fog and recent -RA at KHAF, OVC027 ceiling, cold SST 12.6°C sustaining deep marine layer, 100% cloud all day

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal site, SW-facing, 2517 ft summit
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a thermal-dominant site with a secondary ridge component. It works best on clear sunny days with W to NW valley breeze developing by late morning, thermals bubbling off the east-facing slopes, and BL heights above 1000m. South winds at the summit create turbulent, crossed conditions.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak is NO FLY today for the same core reason as Ed Levin: relentless 100% overcast eliminates all thermal development, and there is no ridge lift compensation on a day with SSW winds. Winds at 10m run SSW at 10–14 kts all day, rotating slowly to W by mid-afternoon — a direction that is crossed for the launch and creates unpredictable flow across the peak's complex terrain. The sounding's saturated profile at 850mb confirms the cloud deck is deep and entrenched. BL tops briefly reach 1275m around 2 PM but that number is meaningless under solid cloud. CAPE hovers at 50–140 J/kg with precip probability reaching 13% at 2 PM, hinting at residual convective instability that could produce turbulent outflow rather than organized thermals. KRHV BKN030 confirms low broken ceilings in the Fremont valley below the site.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast, SSW at 11–13 kts, BL 430–565m. No solar heating, no thermals, low ceiling. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds drift SW to W at 11–14 kts, BL briefly 1275m at 2 PM but fully cloud-covered, precip risk 7–13%. Disorganized air with CAPE instability. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds veer toward WNW at 10–11 kts, BL collapses to 375–625m, cloud 98–100%. No improvement, thermals dead. Not flyable.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast all day, no thermals, SSW wind crossed for launch, residual CAPE with precip risk up to 13%, low ceilings per KRHV BKN030

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Thursday looks dramatically better. GFS and HRRR agree on clearing skies (0–37% cloud), N to NNE winds at 10–13 kts at launch, and BL heights climbing to 925–1025m by afternoon. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 18–19 kts at the 600 ft level, suggesting punchy thermals possible. Winds are from a slightly cross direction (N to NNE vs ideal W-NW) — worth watching, but thermal activity should be the dominant lift source. P3 day with active thermals; P2 pilots should assess carefully.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Clear skies and N-NNE winds 9–12 kts with gusts to 18 kts per Tomorrow.io. BL reaches 1150m by mid-afternoon. Morning may have residual cloud at 100% per Tomorrow.io (conflicting with HRRR's near-zero cloud), so burn-off timing is uncertain — wait for HRRR updates in the morning. If it clears, midday-to-afternoon thermal window looks workable for P2+ with moderate punch.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Thursday shows a significant wind direction shift to NW (322–339°) at 10–15 kts, BL heights reaching 945–1265m, and mostly clear skies. This is the ideal direction for Mussel Rock. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 19–21 kts, which is on the stronger side but within the site's flyable range. Best window looks like 10 AM–4 PM. P3 conditions with punchy ridge lift.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to Ed Levin — clear skies return, N to NNE winds at 10–13 kts, BL 690–1025m. Tomorrow.io flags gusts to 15–17 kts. The northerly direction is slightly cross for Mission Peak's SW-facing launch, so thermal reliance will be key. Viable P3 window likely 11 AM–3 PM if thermals organize.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Friday (Day 2) GFS shows very light winds (5–7 kts NNW) with BL heights climbing to 980–1085m and clear skies. Light and variable flow could mean silky smooth thermals or unpredictable drift. Classic light-day setup — excellent for experienced thermal pilots, potentially tricky for P2s with weak cues.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same light-wind setup as high launch. 5–7 kts NNW with clear skies and BL to 1085m. Gentle thermal day. A morning early start (NE at 18 kts per GFS at 8 AM) suggests residual drainage flow — wait for it to settle before launching.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Friday shows very light winds (3–8 kts from E/NE early, shifting to light W-NW by afternoon). Not enough ridge lift for Mussel Rock. BL to 1115m under clear skies suggests a thermal component could develop, but Mussel Rock isn't a reliable thermal site. Likely marginal at best.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Light NNE winds (5–14 kts) with BL to 970m and clear skies. Good thermal day structurally. Morning drainage winds from NE at 14 kts could provide a brief ridge component before the sea breeze fills in from SW. Promising P2-P3 thermal window in the midday hours.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Saturday) data is limited but early signals show light NNE flow transitioning to SW by midday. If the pattern holds, a classic thermal morning transitioning to sea-breeze afternoon is possible. Monitor the forecast as it comes into range.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Similar to high launch — light variable flow, potentially good thermal day if the pattern from Day 2 extends. Confidence low at this range.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Very limited data. GFS shows calm to light flow early, possibly shifting to light W-NW afternoon. Could be a light soarable day if sea breeze fills in, but confidence is low at 3-day range.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Light NNE to N winds with clear skies hinted at Day 3. If the pattern holds, another thermal window is possible. Too early to be specific — check updated models Thursday morning.

watchlist: Marine layer recovery: cold SST (12.6°C at 46012 Half Moon Bay) and post-frontal moisture could cause tomorrow's clearing to arrive later than forecast at coastal sites — check morning KHAF METAR before heading to Mussel Rock
Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 19–21 kts at Mussel Rock Thursday — strong for a ridge site, could push P2 pilots beyond comfort; verify with morning models
Ed Levin Thursday morning cloud burn-off timing is uncertain — Tomorrow.io shows 100% cloud at 8 AM vs HRRR near-clear; don't leave early, wait for visual confirmation of clearing
KRHV and KAPC showed recent precip today with post-frontal instability; residual moisture could linger into Thursday morning for inland sites
tldr: Today is a wash across all four sites — a thick post-frontal overcast with 100% cloud cover, no solar heating, and recent light rain kills thermals everywhere and leaves Mussel Rock with wrong-direction SSW flow. Thursday is the day to fly: skies clear, NW winds fill in at Mussel Rock (best window 10 AM–4 PM), and Ed Levin/Mission Peak get thermal conditions with BL to 1000m+. Sit tight today and prep for Thursday.
